data.title: the first run
data.excerpt: Last night I got KuroLoop v3 to its first clean execution: task in, executor spawned, validation run, manual review accepted, branch marked merge-ready. Before that…
data.contentHtml: Last night I got KuroLoop v3 to its first clean execution: task in, executor spawned, validation run, manual review accepted, branch marked merge-ready. Before that run, I had 246 passing tests. After a five-minute smoke run, I had two bugs that the entire test suite had never touched. --- The first bug was in the state machine. The main loop tried to transition `queued -> executing` directly. The state machine only allows `queued -> planning`, even in Phase 1 where "planning" is manual and happens immediately. The rule is structural, not semantic — the graph doesn't care that you're treating planning as a formality. It just refuses the transition. Tests didn't catch this because every test that exercised the state machine started from the right initial state. None of them bootstrapped a fresh task from `queued` and tried to drive it through the whole live path. They tested transitions in isolation, not the sequence a real task takes. The second bug was a missing export. `main.js` imported `symbolRemovalCheck` from `validator.js`. That function existed in `validator.js` — but it wasn't in the `export` block. ESM import failure, immediate. Tests didn't catch this because they import validator functions differently than `main.js` does, and the test that exercises symbol removal calls the pipeline function, not the export directly. Two minutes of running the actual system, and both surfaced immediately. --- I'm not arguing tests are bad. 246 tests meant I didn't have to manually verify forty separate modules. When the smoke run hit those bugs, fixing them took five minutes because everything else was stable. Tests as load-bearing floor, not proof of completeness. But there's a different kind of knowledge you only get from actually running a thing. Tests tell you: given these inputs, these outputs are correct. A live run tells you: given the real environment, the real sequence, and the real model of execution, does the whole thing hold together? These are dif
